questions.correctAnswer: B) Constantinople
questions.explanation: B) Constantinople

questions.explanation: A) Vasco da Gama

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is meant by Blue Water Policy?
questions.correctAnswer: The policy of strengthening naval power to monopolize the sea instead of dominating the land is called the Blue Water Policy.
questions.explanation: The policy of strengthening naval power to monopolize the sea instead of dominating the land is called the Blue Water Policy.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Who was the real founder of the Portuguese Empire in India?
questions.correctAnswer: Alfonso de Albuquerque
questions.explanation: Alfonso de Albuquerque

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Discuss the factors that led to the discovery of a new sea route to India.
questions.correctAnswer: The Turks captured Constantinople and began imposing taxes on trade, making it unprofitable for merchants. European kings encouraged sailors to explore. Scientific inventions like the compass, astrolabe, and gunpowder helped exploration. The desire to break Arab and Turkish monopoly over trade also motivated discovery.
questions.explanation: The Turks captured Constantinople and began imposing taxes on trade, making it unprofitable for merchants. European kings encouraged sailors to explore. Scientific inventions like the compass, astrolabe, and gunpowder helped exploration. The desire to break Arab and Turkish monopoly over trade also motivated discovery.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the system of 'Dual Government' in Bengal.
questions.correctAnswer: Robert Clive introduced Dual Government in Bengal. The British had the right to collect land revenue (Diwani rights). The Nawab of Bengal performed administrative functions such as civil administration and justice. This gave British economic control while maintaining Indian facade of rule.
questions.explanation: Robert Clive introduced Dual Government in Bengal. The British had the right to collect land revenue (Diwani rights). The Nawab of Bengal performed administrative functions such as civil administration and justice. This gave British economic control while maintaining Indian facade of rule.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What were the causes and consequences of the Battle of Plassey?
questions.correctAnswer: Causes: Misuse of trade privileges by British employees; Siraj-ud-Daula demanded British to stop fortifying; British supported his rivals; repairing fort without permission. Consequences: British gained political power in Bengal; Robert Clive became Governor of Bengal; Mir Jafar became Nawab; British became dominant power in Bengal; paved way for further expansion.
questions.explanation: Causes: Misuse of trade privileges by British employees; Siraj-ud-Daula demanded British to stop fortifying; British supported his rivals; repairing fort without permission. Consequences: British gained political power in Bengal; Robert Clive became Governor of Bengal; Mir Jafar became Nawab; British became dominant power in Bengal; paved way for further expansion.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The battle of Buxar helped the British to gain control over Bengal. Justify.
questions.correctAnswer: Shah Alam II granted East India Company the Diwani right over Bengal. Shah Alam had to give up his right over Bengal after receiving an annual salary of 26 lakh rupees. The company had to pay 50 lakh rupees as war compensation. Mir Zafar's son was given a pension. The complete administration of Bengal came under company control. Robert Clive introduced Dual Government. British became real holders of power over Bihar, Bengal and Odisha.
questions.explanation: Shah Alam II granted East India Company the Diwani right over Bengal. Shah Alam had to give up his right over Bengal after receiving an annual salary of 26 lakh rupees. The company had to pay 50 lakh rupees as war compensation. Mir Zafar's son was given a pension. The complete administration of Bengal came under company control. Robert Clive introduced Dual Government. British became real holders of power over Bihar, Bengal and Odisha.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The First Anglo-Maratha War ended with the treaty of
questions.correctAnswer: B) Salbai
questions.explanation: B) Salbai

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The system of Subsidiary Alliance was introduced by
questions.correctAnswer: B) Wellesley
questions.explanation: B) Wellesley

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The first state which accepted the system of Subsidiary Alliance was
questions.correctAnswer: A) Hyderabad
questions.explanation: A) Hyderabad

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What were the conditions of Subsidiary Alliance? / The Subsidiary Alliance weakened the native rulers. How?
questions.correctAnswer: The Indian king should keep a British military force in his state. The state should bear the cost of the army's salary and maintenance. A British resident should be appointed in the court. No European could be appointed at the court without British permission. Consent of British was required for war or negotiations with any state. The company should provide internal and external defense. This weakened rulers by: draining treasury for army costs, placing British spies as residents, removing independent military power, and making rulers dependent on British.
questions.explanation: The Indian king should keep a British military force in his state. The state should bear the cost of the army's salary and maintenance. A British resident should be appointed in the court. No European could be appointed at the court without British permission. Consent of British was required for war or negotiations with any state. The company should provide internal and external defense. This weakened rulers by: draining treasury for army costs, placing British spies as residents, removing independent military power, and making rulers dependent on British.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: How did the Doctrine of Lapse support the expansion of British Empire in India?
questions.correctAnswer: Dalhousie implemented the Doctrine of Lapse. As per this, if a ruler died without a natural heir, the state would be annexed by the British. Adopted children had no succession rights. States like Satara, Jaipur, Sambalpur, Udaipur, Jhansi and Nagpur came under British control through this policy.
questions.explanation: Dalhousie implemented the Doctrine of Lapse. As per this, if a ruler died without a natural heir, the state would be annexed by the British. Adopted children had no succession rights. States like Satara, Jaipur, Sambalpur, Udaipur, Jhansi and Nagpur came under British control through this policy.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.chapter: Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables
questions.question: How does the Government of India Act of 1935 become the base of the Indian Constitution?
questions.correctAnswer: The Government of India Act 1935 laid foundation for the Constitution of India. It formed an all-India Federation. A dual government (dyarchy) was established at the centre. Provinces were given autonomy. Reserve Bank of India was established. Federal Court was established in India. Most provisions of the Indian Constitution are based on this act. It allowed formation of fully responsible government by Indians.
questions.explanation: The Government of India Act 1935 laid foundation for the Constitution of India. It formed an all-India Federation. A dual government (dyarchy) was established at the centre. Provinces were given autonomy. Reserve Bank of India was established. Federal Court was established in India. Most provisions of the Indian Constitution are based on this act. It allowed formation of fully responsible government by Indians.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.chapter: Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables
questions.question: What were the important features of the 1919 Government of India Act (Montague-Chelmsford Reforms)?
questions.correctAnswer: A bicameral legislature was formed at the Centre. A dual system of government (dyarchy) was introduced in the provinces. A High Commissioner was appointed for India. Development of local bodies was encouraged. Budget of provinces was separated from central budget. Separate electoral constituencies were extended to Sikhs, Anglo-Indians and Europeans.
questions.explanation: A bicameral legislature was formed at the Centre. A dual system of government (dyarchy) was introduced in the provinces. A High Commissioner was appointed for India. Development of local bodies was encouraged. Budget of provinces was separated from central budget. Separate electoral constituencies were extended to Sikhs, Anglo-Indians and Europeans.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The teacher and mentor of Krishnaraja Wadiyar was
questions.correctAnswer: A) Basappa Shastri
questions.explanation: A) Basappa Shastri

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Who was Sangolli Rayanna?
questions.correctAnswer: Sangolli Rayanna was a brave commander who fought alongside Rani Chennamma in the Kittur Rebellion against the British. He continued the struggle even after Rani Chennamma's imprisonment and became a symbol of resistance.
questions.explanation: Sangolli Rayanna was a brave commander who fought alongside Rani Chennamma in the Kittur Rebellion against the British. He continued the struggle even after Rani Chennamma's imprisonment and became a symbol of resistance.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the contributions of Hyder Ali and Tipu Sultan in opposing British rule.
questions.correctAnswer: Hyder Ali: Built a strong army with French support. Introduced iron rockets in warfare. Won the First Anglo-Mysore War (1767-69). Defeated British at Pollilur during Second Anglo-Mysore War. Tipu Sultan: Called 'Tiger of Mysore'. Continued wars against British. Used rockets effectively against British. Fought four Anglo-Mysore Wars. Built Lal Mahal, Srirangapatna fort. Died fighting the British in 1799 at the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War. Both were strong opponents of British expansion in South India.
questions.explanation: Hyder Ali: Built a strong army with French support. Introduced iron rockets in warfare. Won the First Anglo-Mysore War (1767-69). Defeated British at Pollilur during Second Anglo-Mysore War. Tipu Sultan: Called 'Tiger of Mysore'. Continued wars against British. Used rockets effectively against British. Fought four Anglo-Mysore Wars. Built Lal Mahal, Srirangapatna fort. Died fighting the British in 1799 at the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War. Both were strong opponents of British expansion in South India.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the Kittur Rebellion led by Rani Chennamma.
questions.correctAnswer: After the death of the king of Kittur, the British tried to annex Kittur through Doctrine of Lapse. Rani Chennamma of Kittur refused to accept British authority and fought against them in 1824. She initially defeated the British and captured their collector Thackeray. She was later captured and imprisoned in Bailhongal fort where she died in 1829. Her bravery inspired future freedom fighters.
questions.explanation: After the death of the king of Kittur, the British tried to annex Kittur through Doctrine of Lapse. Rani Chennamma of Kittur refused to accept British authority and fought against them in 1824. She initially defeated the British and captured their collector Thackeray. She was later captured and imprisoned in Bailhongal fort where she died in 1829. Her bravery inspired future freedom fighters.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the achievements and contributions of Krishnaraja Wodeyar IV.
questions.correctAnswer: Krishnaraja Wadiyar IV ruled Mysore from 1902 to 1940. He was educated under Basappa Shastri. He implemented several reforms: established Vidhana Soudha, developed Krishnarajasagara Dam with Diwan Visvesvaraya, promoted industries like silk, sandalwood, etc. He was known as Rajarshi (Royal Saint). He promoted education, art and culture. He established Mysore Bank. He supported the Indian independence movement discreetly. He was respected by Mahatma Gandhi. He transformed Mysore into one of the most progressive states in India.
questions.explanation: Krishnaraja Wadiyar IV ruled Mysore from 1902 to 1940. He was educated under Basappa Shastri. He implemented several reforms: established Vidhana Soudha, developed Krishnarajasagara Dam with Diwan Visvesvaraya, promoted industries like silk, sandalwood, etc. He was known as Rajarshi (Royal Saint). He promoted education, art and culture. He established Mysore Bank. He supported the Indian independence movement discreetly. He was respected by Mahatma Gandhi. He transformed Mysore into one of the most progressive states in India.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Brahma Samaj was founded by
questions.correctAnswer: C) Raja Ram Mohan Roy
questions.explanation: C) Raja Ram Mohan Roy

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Why is the 19th century known as the period of 'Indian Renaissance'?
questions.correctAnswer: The 19th century is called the period of Indian Renaissance because it saw a great awakening in India. Social reformers fought against caste discrimination, sati, child marriage and untouchability. Religious reformers purified Indian religion. New organizations were established. Education spread. Indians began questioning superstitions and working for social equality.
questions.explanation: The 19th century is called the period of Indian Renaissance because it saw a great awakening in India. Social reformers fought against caste discrimination, sati, child marriage and untouchability. Religious reformers purified Indian religion. New organizations were established. Education spread. Indians began questioning superstitions and working for social equality.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the contribution of any two social reform organizations of India.
questions.correctAnswer: 1. Brahma Samaj (1828): Founded by Raja Ram Mohan Roy. Opposed idol worship, caste system and sati. Promoted monotheism and rational thinking. Fought for widow remarriage. Led to abolition of Sati in 1829. 2. Arya Samaj (1875): Founded by Dayananda Saraswathi. Rejected idol worship and casteism. Based on Vedic principles. Promoted education, especially for women. Opposed untouchability. Shuddhi movement to reconvert people. Both contributed greatly to social awakening and laid foundation for nationalism.
questions.explanation: 1. Brahma Samaj (1828): Founded by Raja Ram Mohan Roy. Opposed idol worship, caste system and sati. Promoted monotheism and rational thinking. Fought for widow remarriage. Led to abolition of Sati in 1829. 2. Arya Samaj (1875): Founded by Dayananda Saraswathi. Rejected idol worship and casteism. Based on Vedic principles. Promoted education, especially for women. Opposed untouchability. Shuddhi movement to reconvert people. Both contributed greatly to social awakening and laid foundation for nationalism.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The immediate cause of the First War of Independence (1857) was
questions.correctAnswer: B) Greased cartridges
questions.explanation: B) Greased cartridges

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What did British historians call the 1857 revolt?
questions.correctAnswer: The Sepoy Mutiny
questions.explanation: The Sepoy Mutiny

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the political and economic causes of the First War of Independence (1857).
questions.correctAnswer: Political Causes: Princely states of Satara, Jaipur, Jhansi, Udaipur were captured through Doctrine of Lapse. Kingship of Thanjavur and Nawabs of Carnatic was abolished. Mughal emperor and Nawab of Oudh were deposed. Millions of soldiers who depended on them became unemployed. Economic Causes: Industrial revolution in England ruined Indian handicrafts. Indian craftsmen became unemployed. Textile and wool industries suffered. British imposed heavy tax on sale of Indian goods. Farmers were burdened with heavy land taxes. Indian agriculture was commercialized to serve British interests.
questions.explanation: Political Causes: Princely states of Satara, Jaipur, Jhansi, Udaipur were captured through Doctrine of Lapse. Kingship of Thanjavur and Nawabs of Carnatic was abolished. Mughal emperor and Nawab of Oudh were deposed. Millions of soldiers who depended on them became unemployed. Economic Causes: Industrial revolution in England ruined Indian handicrafts. Indian craftsmen became unemployed. Textile and wool industries suffered. British imposed heavy tax on sale of Indian goods. Farmers were burdened with heavy land taxes. Indian agriculture was commercialized to serve British interests.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What were the features of the declaration made by the British Queen in 1858?
questions.correctAnswer: The agreements made by the British East India Company with kings were accepted. Ambitious expansion plans had to be given up. A stable government had to be provided to Indians. There would be equality before law. The government would exhibit religious tolerance and not interfere in religious matters. Administration of India passed from the East India Company to the British Crown.
questions.explanation: The agreements made by the British East India Company with kings were accepted. Ambitious expansion plans had to be given up. A stable government had to be provided to Indians. There would be equality before law. The government would exhibit religious tolerance and not interfere in religious matters. Administration of India passed from the East India Company to the British Crown.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The Indian National Congress was founded by
questions.correctAnswer: B) A.O. Hume
questions.explanation: B) A.O. Hume

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: In which year was the Indian National Congress established?

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the Civil Disobedience Movement.
questions.correctAnswer: The Civil Disobedience Movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1930. He marched from Sabarmati to Dandi (Dandi March) to break the Salt Law. People across India boycotted British goods, refused to pay taxes, and violated British laws deliberately. Many leaders like Gandhi, Nehru, Sardar Patel were imprisoned. The movement spread widely. It was suspended after Gandhi-Irwin Pact in 1931. It demonstrated the power of non-violent mass resistance and brought international attention to India's freedom struggle.
questions.explanation: The Civil Disobedience Movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1930. He marched from Sabarmati to Dandi (Dandi March) to break the Salt Law. People across India boycotted British goods, refused to pay taxes, and violated British laws deliberately. Many leaders like Gandhi, Nehru, Sardar Patel were imprisoned. The movement spread widely. It was suspended after Gandhi-Irwin Pact in 1931. It demonstrated the power of non-violent mass resistance and brought international attention to India's freedom struggle.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the Quit India Movement.
questions.correctAnswer: The Quit India Movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi on August 8, 1942. Gandhi gave the call 'Do or Die'. Leaders like Gandhi, Nehru, Rajendra Prasad, Abul Kalam Azad, Sardar Patel were imprisoned immediately. Since Congress leaders were in jail, new leaders emerged including Jayaprakash Narayan. The movement spread across India. British used force to suppress it. Subhash Chandra Bose's INA activities complemented this from outside India. The movement accelerated India's path to independence.
questions.explanation: The Quit India Movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi on August 8, 1942. Gandhi gave the call 'Do or Die'. Leaders like Gandhi, Nehru, Rajendra Prasad, Abul Kalam Azad, Sardar Patel were imprisoned immediately. Since Congress leaders were in jail, new leaders emerged including Jayaprakash Narayan. The movement spread across India. British used force to suppress it. Subhash Chandra Bose's INA activities complemented this from outside India. The movement accelerated India's path to independence.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the role of Moderates and Extremists in the Indian freedom struggle.
questions.correctAnswer: Moderates (1885-1905): Represented by Dadabai Naoroji, M.G. Ranade, Gopal Krishna Gokhale, Surendranath Banerjee. Believed in British justice and rule. Presented demands through prayers and petitions. Provided political education. Reduced military expenditure demands. Studied poverty. Demanded relief programs. Extremists (1905-1920): Represented by Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Bipin Chandra Pal, Lala Lajpat Rai (Lal-Bal-Pal). Demanded complete self-rule (Swaraj). Used mass agitation. Boycott of British goods. Used religious festivals to mobilize people. Tilak used Kesari and Maratha newspapers. Believed that rights must be seized, not begged. Both phases contributed to building a strong independence movement.
questions.explanation: Moderates (1885-1905): Represented by Dadabai Naoroji, M.G. Ranade, Gopal Krishna Gokhale, Surendranath Banerjee. Believed in British justice and rule. Presented demands through prayers and petitions. Provided political education. Reduced military expenditure demands. Studied poverty. Demanded relief programs. Extremists (1905-1920): Represented by Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Bipin Chandra Pal, Lala Lajpat Rai (Lal-Bal-Pal). Demanded complete self-rule (Swaraj). Used mass agitation. Boycott of British goods. Used religious festivals to mobilize people. Tilak used Kesari and Maratha newspapers. Believed that rights must be seized, not begged. Both phases contributed to building a strong independence movement.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The States Reorganisation Act was passed in the year

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What were the problems faced by India after independence?
questions.correctAnswer: India faced the following problems after independence: Partition of India led to communal violence and refugee crisis. Integration of princely states was a major challenge — Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel integrated over 562 states. Formation of a stable government was complex. States were reorganized on linguistic basis. Poverty, unemployment and economic underdevelopment were major challenges. Disputes with Pakistan over Kashmir began.
questions.explanation: India faced the following problems after independence: Partition of India led to communal violence and refugee crisis. Integration of princely states was a major challenge — Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel integrated over 562 states. Formation of a stable government was complex. States were reorganized on linguistic basis. Poverty, unemployment and economic underdevelopment were major challenges. Disputes with Pakistan over Kashmir began.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The countries that were involved in the Cold War after the Second World War were
questions.correctAnswer: C) USA and Soviet Russia
questions.explanation: C) USA and Soviet Russia

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the causes of the First World War.
questions.correctAnswer: Causes: Aggressive nationalism in European countries. Alliance system divided Europe into two camps (Triple Alliance and Triple Entente). Colonial rivalries for markets and raw materials. Arms race between major powers. Militarism — glorification of military power. Immediate cause: Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria in Sarajevo (1914) by Gavrilo Princip triggered the war.
questions.explanation: Causes: Aggressive nationalism in European countries. Alliance system divided Europe into two camps (Triple Alliance and Triple Entente). Colonial rivalries for markets and raw materials. Arms race between major powers. Militarism — glorification of military power. Immediate cause: Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria in Sarajevo (1914) by Gavrilo Princip triggered the war.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The central body that conducts recruitment examinations for central government services is
questions.correctAnswer: B) UPSC
questions.explanation: B) UPSC

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is public administration? What is its scope?
questions.correctAnswer: Public Administration refers to the organization and management of government activities and policies. It includes planning, organizing, directing and controlling activities of the government. Scope includes: civil services, police administration, revenue collection, welfare schemes, judicial administration, public health, education, defense, and local body administration. It is essential for implementing government programs and maintaining law and order.
questions.explanation: Public Administration refers to the organization and management of government activities and policies. It includes planning, organizing, directing and controlling activities of the government. Scope includes: civil services, police administration, revenue collection, welfare schemes, judicial administration, public health, education, defense, and local body administration. It is essential for implementing government programs and maintaining law and order.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The institution established at the national level to control corruption is
questions.correctAnswer: B) Lokpal
questions.explanation: B) Lokpal

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What are the measures taken by the Government of India to control corruption?
questions.correctAnswer: Government measures to control corruption: Lokpal established at national level; Lokayukta in states. Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) set up. Prevention of Corruption Act 1988 implemented. Right to Information Act 2005 (RTI) — citizens can seek information from government. CC Cameras installed in government offices. E-Office system implemented for quick file disposal. Schemes like Sakala, Janaspandana, Janasevaka implemented. Complaint boxes kept in government offices. Stricter penal systems for corrupt officials.
questions.explanation: Government measures to control corruption: Lokpal established at national level; Lokayukta in states. Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) set up. Prevention of Corruption Act 1988 implemented. Right to Information Act 2005 (RTI) — citizens can seek information from government. CC Cameras installed in government offices. E-Office system implemented for quick file disposal. Schemes like Sakala, Janaspandana, Janasevaka implemented. Complaint boxes kept in government offices. Stricter penal systems for corrupt officials.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is communalism? What are the measures to control it?
questions.correctAnswer: Communalism is excessive attachment to one's religious community leading to hostility toward other communities. Measures to control: Promotion of national integration through education. Strict enforcement of law against communal violence. Promotion of inter-religious harmony. Media responsibility. Government schemes for minority welfare. Ban on organizations promoting religious hatred. Strong punishment for those spreading communal disharmony.
questions.explanation: Communalism is excessive attachment to one's religious community leading to hostility toward other communities. Measures to control: Promotion of national integration through education. Strict enforcement of law against communal violence. Promotion of inter-religious harmony. Media responsibility. Government schemes for minority welfare. Ban on organizations promoting religious hatred. Strong punishment for those spreading communal disharmony.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is the foreign policy of India called?
questions.correctAnswer: India's foreign policy is based on the principle of Non-Alignment (NAM - Non-Aligned Movement). It is also called Panchsheel Policy based on five principles of peaceful coexistence.
questions.explanation: India's foreign policy is based on the principle of Non-Alignment (NAM - Non-Aligned Movement). It is also called Panchsheel Policy based on five principles of peaceful coexistence.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the fundamental principles of India's foreign policy.
questions.correctAnswer: India's foreign policy is based on: 1. Non-Alignment: Not joining any military bloc. 2. Panchsheel: Five principles — mutual respect for sovereignty, non-aggression, non-interference in internal affairs, equality and mutual benefit, peaceful coexistence. 3. World peace and disarmament. 4. Opposition to colonialism and imperialism. 5. Support for United Nations. 6. Promotion of South-South cooperation. 7. Nuclear disarmament. 8. Respect for international law. These principles were shaped by Jawaharlal Nehru.
questions.explanation: India's foreign policy is based on: 1. Non-Alignment: Not joining any military bloc. 2. Panchsheel: Five principles — mutual respect for sovereignty, non-aggression, non-interference in internal affairs, equality and mutual benefit, peaceful coexistence. 3. World peace and disarmament. 4. Opposition to colonialism and imperialism. 5. Support for United Nations. 6. Promotion of South-South cooperation. 7. Nuclear disarmament. 8. Respect for international law. These principles were shaped by Jawaharlal Nehru.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The headquarters of the World Health Organization is in
questions.correctAnswer: C) Geneva
questions.explanation: C) Geneva

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What are the main organs of the United Nations?
questions.correctAnswer: The United Nations has six main organs: 1. General Assembly — all member nations have equal vote. 2. Security Council — 5 permanent members (USA, UK, France, Russia, China) and 10 non-permanent members. 3. Secretariat — headed by Secretary General. 4. International Court of Justice — at The Hague. 5. Economic and Social Council. 6. Trusteeship Council (inactive since 1994). The UN aims to maintain international peace and security.
questions.explanation: The United Nations has six main organs: 1. General Assembly — all member nations have equal vote. 2. Security Council — 5 permanent members (USA, UK, France, Russia, China) and 10 non-permanent members. 3. Secretariat — headed by Secretary General. 4. International Court of Justice — at The Hague. 5. Economic and Social Council. 6. Trusteeship Council (inactive since 1994). The UN aims to maintain international peace and security.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The division of society into hierarchical groups based on caste, class or gender is called
questions.correctAnswer: B) Social stratification
questions.explanation: B) Social stratification

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is untouchability?
questions.correctAnswer: Untouchability is a social evil practiced in India where certain castes were treated as impure and denied basic social rights. They were prevented from accessing wells, temples, schools, and were forced to do menial work. It was declared illegal under Article 17 of the Indian Constitution.
questions.explanation: Untouchability is a social evil practiced in India where certain castes were treated as impure and denied basic social rights. They were prevented from accessing wells, temples, schools, and were forced to do menial work. It was declared illegal under Article 17 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Give an account of the achievements of Subhash Chandra Bose in the independence struggle.
questions.correctAnswer: Subhash Chandra Bose (Netaji): Born in 1897. Joined INC and became president twice (1938, 1939). Disagreed with Gandhi on violent vs non-violent approach. Escaped from house arrest in 1941. Reached Germany and then Japan. Formed Indian National Army (INA/Azad Hind Fauj) in 1943. Established Azad Hind Government. Led INA troops from Singapore to Manipur (Imphal campaign 1944). INA soldiers fought with slogan 'Jai Hind'. Gave the call 'Give me blood, I will give you freedom.' INA trials after WWII sparked massive nationalist sentiment across India. His activities demonstrated India's determination for freedom internationally.
questions.explanation: Subhash Chandra Bose (Netaji): Born in 1897. Joined INC and became president twice (1938, 1939). Disagreed with Gandhi on violent vs non-violent approach. Escaped from house arrest in 1941. Reached Germany and then Japan. Formed Indian National Army (INA/Azad Hind Fauj) in 1943. Established Azad Hind Government. Led INA troops from Singapore to Manipur (Imphal campaign 1944). INA soldiers fought with slogan 'Jai Hind'. Gave the call 'Give me blood, I will give you freedom.' INA trials after WWII sparked massive nationalist sentiment across India. His activities demonstrated India's determination for freedom internationally.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the struggle and contributions of Dr. B.R. Ambedkar in India's freedom struggle.
questions.correctAnswer: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar (1891−1956): Belonged to the Mahar caste, faced severe discrimination. Studied in USA and UK, became a great scholar. Fought against untouchability and caste discrimination. Founded Bahishkrit Hitakarini Sabha (1924) for depressed classes. Led Mahad Tank Satyagraha (1927) for water rights. Led Kalaram Temple entry movement (1930). Participated in all three Round Table Conferences. Demanded separate electorate for depressed classes (Poona Pact 1932 with Gandhi). Founded Independent Labour Party. Converted to Buddhism in 1956. Chief Architect of Indian Constitution as Chairman of Drafting Committee. His contributions are foundational to Indian democracy and social justice.
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar (1891−1956): Belonged to the Mahar caste, faced severe discrimination. Studied in USA and UK, became a great scholar. Fought against untouchability and caste discrimination. Founded Bahishkrit Hitakarini Sabha (1924) for depressed classes. Led Mahad Tank Satyagraha (1927) for water rights. Led Kalaram Temple entry movement (1930). Participated in all three Round Table Conferences. Demanded separate electorate for depressed classes (Poona Pact 1932 with Gandhi). Founded Independent Labour Party. Converted to Buddhism in 1956. Chief Architect of Indian Constitution as Chairman of Drafting Committee. His contributions are foundational to Indian democracy and social justice.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What were the achievements of Jawaharlal Nehru?
questions.correctAnswer: Jawaharlal Nehru (1889−1964): First Prime Minister of India. Studied at Harrow, Cambridge and Inner Temple London. Joined INC under Gandhiji's inspiration. Led Non-Cooperation, Civil Disobedience, and Quit India movements. Imprisoned multiple times. Key role in framing Indian Constitution. As PM: Shaped India's foreign policy (Non-Alignment). Established IITs, IIMs for technological education. Developed nuclear program. Built steel plants, dams — 'temples of modern India'. Established Planning Commission for economic development. Championed secularism and democracy. Founded NAM (1961) with Nasser and Tito. Champion of world peace.
questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru (1889−1964): First Prime Minister of India. Studied at Harrow, Cambridge and Inner Temple London. Joined INC under Gandhiji's inspiration. Led Non-Cooperation, Civil Disobedience, and Quit India movements. Imprisoned multiple times. Key role in framing Indian Constitution. As PM: Shaped India's foreign policy (Non-Alignment). Established IITs, IIMs for technological education. Developed nuclear program. Built steel plants, dams — 'temples of modern India'. Established Planning Commission for economic development. Championed secularism and democracy. Founded NAM (1961) with Nasser and Tito. Champion of world peace.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the major challenges faced by India and suggest remedies.
questions.correctAnswer: 1. Communalism: Religious divisions; remedy: education, strict laws, promotion of harmony. 2. Regionalism: Demand for separate states; remedy: equal development of all regions, linguistic rights. 3. Illiteracy: Large uneducated population; remedy: RTE Act, Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan, skill development. 4. Gender Discrimination: Women face inequality; remedy: education, laws against dowry/child marriage, political representation. 5. Corruption: Misuse of public office; remedy: Lokpal, RTI, e-governance, strong penalties. 6. Poverty: Large population below poverty line; remedy: MGNREGA, food security, housing schemes. 7. Terrorism: Internal security threats; remedy: strong intelligence, international cooperation, addressing root causes.
questions.explanation: 1. Communalism: Religious divisions; remedy: education, strict laws, promotion of harmony. 2. Regionalism: Demand for separate states; remedy: equal development of all regions, linguistic rights. 3. Illiteracy: Large uneducated population; remedy: RTE Act, Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan, skill development. 4. Gender Discrimination: Women face inequality; remedy: education, laws against dowry/child marriage, political representation. 5. Corruption: Misuse of public office; remedy: Lokpal, RTI, e-governance, strong penalties. 6. Poverty: Large population below poverty line; remedy: MGNREGA, food security, housing schemes. 7. Terrorism: Internal security threats; remedy: strong intelligence, international cooperation, addressing root causes.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What were the teachings and objectives of the Ramakrishna Mission?
questions.correctAnswer: Ramakrishna Mission founded by Swami Vivekananda in 1897. Teachings: All religions lead to the same God. Service to man is service to God ('Shiva Jnane Jeeva Seva'). Harmony among all religions. Practical Vedanta. Objectives: Social service — education, hospitals, disaster relief. Spreading Vedanta philosophy. Women's education. Fighting superstition. Establishing schools and colleges. It combined spiritual and social reform.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What was the role of India in the Second World War?
questions.correctAnswer: India's role in World War II: British India was declared at war in 1939 without consulting Indian leaders. Over 2.5 million Indians served in British forces. INC resigned from ministries in protest. Subhash Chandra Bose formed INA to fight British with Japanese support. Quit India Movement (1942) launched demanding independence. India was a major supply base for Allied forces. The war exposed British weakness and accelerated Indian independence movement, leading to independence in 1947.
questions.explanation: India's role in World War II: British India was declared at war in 1939 without consulting Indian leaders. Over 2.5 million Indians served in British forces. INC resigned from ministries in protest. Subhash Chandra Bose formed INA to fight British with Japanese support. Quit India Movement (1942) launched demanding independence. India was a major supply base for Allied forces. The war exposed British weakness and accelerated Indian independence movement, leading to independence in 1947.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What are the consequences of terrorism? How can it be controlled?
questions.correctAnswer: Consequences: Loss of innocent lives. Economic damage — disrupts tourism, investment. Political instability. Fear and social unrest. Strained international relations. Human rights violations. Displacement of populations. Control measures: Strong intelligence network. International cooperation (UN resolutions). Cutting financing of terrorist groups. Addressing root causes — poverty, inequality. Education to prevent radicalization. Strict anti-terrorism laws. India's POTA, UAPA for counter-terrorism. Regional cooperation through SAARC.
questions.explanation: Consequences: Loss of innocent lives. Economic damage — disrupts tourism, investment. Political instability. Fear and social unrest. Strained international relations. Human rights violations. Displacement of populations. Control measures: Strong intelligence network. International cooperation (UN resolutions). Cutting financing of terrorist groups. Addressing root causes — poverty, inequality. Education to prevent radicalization. Strict anti-terrorism laws. India's POTA, UAPA for counter-terrorism. Regional cooperation through SAARC.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What were the consequences of the Second Carnatic War?
questions.correctAnswer: Ended with Treaty of Pondicherry. The French recalled Dupleix back to France. It gave the French a political setback. British gained political prestige. British emerged stronger after the Second Carnatic War.
questions.explanation: Ended with Treaty of Pondicherry. The French recalled Dupleix back to France. It gave the French a political setback. British gained political prestige. British emerged stronger after the Second Carnatic War.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Which states came under the Doctrine of Lapse policy?
questions.correctAnswer: States absorbed: Satara (1848), Jaipur, Sambalpur (1849), Udaipur (1852), Jhansi (1853), Nagpur (1854). Adopted children were denied succession rights.
questions.explanation: States absorbed: Satara (1848), Jaipur, Sambalpur (1849), Udaipur (1852), Jhansi (1853), Nagpur (1854). Adopted children were denied succession rights.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What were the methods used by Moderates in the Indian National Congress?
questions.correctAnswer: Moderates used petitions and prayers to government. Led by Dadabai Naoroji, Gokhale. Believed in British justice. Provided political education. Demanded reduced military expenditure. Studied poverty. Demanded relief programs. Believed British would grant rights peacefully.
questions.explanation: Moderates used petitions and prayers to government. Led by Dadabai Naoroji, Gokhale. Believed in British justice. Provided political education. Demanded reduced military expenditure. Studied poverty. Demanded relief programs. Believed British would grant rights peacefully.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is the difference between direct recruitment and promotion in public administration?
questions.correctAnswer: Direct recruitment: Selected through competitive examinations or interviews (UPSC, KPSC). No prior government service required. Promotion/Indirect recruitment: Government employees promoted from lower posts based on seniority and merit. Experience in government service required.
questions.explanation: Direct recruitment: Selected through competitive examinations or interviews (UPSC, KPSC). No prior government service required. Promotion/Indirect recruitment: Government employees promoted from lower posts based on seniority and merit. Experience in government service required.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is regionalism? How does it affect national unity?
questions.correctAnswer: Regionalism: Excessive love for one's own region over the nation, leading to demands for separate states. Effects: Creates divisions. Conflicts between states over resources, water, borders. Hinders national integration. Promotes sub-national loyalties.
questions.explanation: Regionalism: Excessive love for one's own region over the nation, leading to demands for separate states. Effects: Creates divisions. Conflicts between states over resources, water, borders. Hinders national integration. Promotes sub-national loyalties.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What are the powers of the UN Security Council?
questions.correctAnswer: Maintain international peace. Investigate disputes. Recommend measures. Call for sanctions. Authorize military force. 5 permanent members have veto power. Can declare ceasefire. Admits new UN members.
questions.explanation: Maintain international peace. Investigate disputes. Recommend measures. Call for sanctions. Authorize military force. 5 permanent members have veto power. Can declare ceasefire. Admits new UN members.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: How did Marthanda Varma control the Dutch in Travancore?
questions.correctAnswer: Marthanda Varma ruled Travancore (1729-1758). Dutch tried to monopolize pepper trade in Kerala. Dutch allied with Kayamkulam, Kochi, Purakkad and Vadakunkur attacked Travancore. Marthanda Varma strongly repelled the attack (Battle of Colachel 1741). Dutch suffered huge losses. He captured trading centers. Strengthened army and administration. Shifted capital to Thiruvananthapuram. Made Travancore a great kingdom resisting Dutch interference. His victory was one of the first defeats of Europeans in India.
questions.explanation: Marthanda Varma ruled Travancore (1729-1758). Dutch tried to monopolize pepper trade in Kerala. Dutch allied with Kayamkulam, Kochi, Purakkad and Vadakunkur attacked Travancore. Marthanda Varma strongly repelled the attack (Battle of Colachel 1741). Dutch suffered huge losses. He captured trading centers. Strengthened army and administration. Shifted capital to Thiruvananthapuram. Made Travancore a great kingdom resisting Dutch interference. His victory was one of the first defeats of Europeans in India.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain Hitler's Nazism and its impact.
questions.correctAnswer: Hitler was dictator of Germany (1933-1945). Nazism features: Extreme nationalism. Racial superiority — Aryans master race; persecuted Jews (Holocaust — 6 million killed). Anti-communism. Totalitarian state. Militarism. Anti-democracy. Propaganda. Impact: Holocaust. Triggered WW2 with invasion of Poland (1939). Occupied most of Europe. Led to UN formation (1945). War crimes trials at Nuremberg. End of European colonial empires. Shaped modern international law.
questions.explanation: Hitler was dictator of Germany (1933-1945). Nazism features: Extreme nationalism. Racial superiority — Aryans master race; persecuted Jews (Holocaust — 6 million killed). Anti-communism. Totalitarian state. Militarism. Anti-democracy. Propaganda. Impact: Holocaust. Triggered WW2 with invasion of Poland (1939). Occupied most of Europe. Led to UN formation (1945). War crimes trials at Nuremberg. End of European colonial empires. Shaped modern international law.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What are the causes and effects of illiteracy in India? What government measures have been taken to eliminate it?
questions.correctAnswer: Causes: Poverty (children need to work). Lack of schools in rural areas. Social customs (girls not educated). Child labour. Physical barriers. Effects: Exploitation. Low productivity. Political manipulation. Poor health. Hindrance to development. Government measures: Right to Education Act 2009 (6-14 years). Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan. Mid-day meal scheme. Rashtriya Madhyamik Shiksha Abhiyan. National Literacy Mission. Digital literacy programs.
questions.explanation: Causes: Poverty (children need to work). Lack of schools in rural areas. Social customs (girls not educated). Child labour. Physical barriers. Effects: Exploitation. Low productivity. Political manipulation. Poor health. Hindrance to development. Government measures: Right to Education Act 2009 (6-14 years). Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan. Mid-day meal scheme. Rashtriya Madhyamik Shiksha Abhiyan. National Literacy Mission. Digital literacy programs.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the causes and effects of the Battle of Buxar (1764).
questions.correctAnswer: Causes: Mir Qasim made all trade duty-free in Bengal to compete with British. British employees misused duty-free privileges. British restored Mir Jafar. Mir Qasim allied with Nawab of Awadh and Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II against British. Battle fought and British won under Hector Munro. Effects: Shah Alam II granted Diwani rights (revenue collection) to East India Company over Bengal, Bihar, Odisha. Company paid Shah Alam 26 lakh annually. Paid 50 lakh as war compensation. Robert Clive introduced Dual Government in Bengal (1765). British became undisputed power in Bengal. More significant than Plassey in establishing British power. Led to complete British domination of eastern India.
questions.explanation: Causes: Mir Qasim made all trade duty-free in Bengal to compete with British. British employees misused duty-free privileges. British restored Mir Jafar. Mir Qasim allied with Nawab of Awadh and Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II against British. Battle fought and British won under Hector Munro. Effects: Shah Alam II granted Diwani rights (revenue collection) to East India Company over Bengal, Bihar, Odisha. Company paid Shah Alam 26 lakh annually. Paid 50 lakh as war compensation. Robert Clive introduced Dual Government in Bengal (1765). British became undisputed power in Bengal. More significant than Plassey in establishing British power. Led to complete British domination of eastern India.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the expansion of British power through the Anglo-Maratha Wars.
questions.correctAnswer: Three Anglo-Maratha Wars: First (1775-1782): British interference in Maratha succession. Treaty of Salbai (1782) — status quo restored. Second (1803-1806): Wellesley used Subsidiary Alliance. British defeated Scindia and Bhonsle. Treaty of Bassein (1802). British gained Surat and Broach. Third (1817-1818): Last stand by Marathas. Peshwa Baji Rao II resisted. British completely defeated Marathas. Peshwa deposed. British gained control of most of India. The Maratha confederacy was destroyed. The Maratha power, once the greatest after Mughals, ended through these wars.
questions.explanation: Three Anglo-Maratha Wars: First (1775-1782): British interference in Maratha succession. Treaty of Salbai (1782) — status quo restored. Second (1803-1806): Wellesley used Subsidiary Alliance. British defeated Scindia and Bhonsle. Treaty of Bassein (1802). British gained Surat and Broach. Third (1817-1818): Last stand by Marathas. Peshwa Baji Rao II resisted. British completely defeated Marathas. Peshwa deposed. British gained control of most of India. The Maratha confederacy was destroyed. The Maratha power, once the greatest after Mughals, ended through these wars.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the rebellion of Halagali Bedas and its significance.
questions.correctAnswer: Halagali Beda Rebellion (1857-58): British Arms Act required Indians to surrender weapons. Beda community of Halagali in Bijapur district depended on weapons for livelihood. Ramappa Nayak led the Bedas in refusal. They fought against British soldiers sent to collect weapons. British suppressed the rebellion with overwhelming force. Ramappa Nayak and leaders were executed. Significance: One of earliest armed revolts in Karnataka. Shows how British policies threatened traditional occupations. Part of broader 1857 resistance. Even common people ready to fight British injustice. Shows how Arms Act disarmed Indian resistance.
questions.explanation: Halagali Beda Rebellion (1857-58): British Arms Act required Indians to surrender weapons. Beda community of Halagali in Bijapur district depended on weapons for livelihood. Ramappa Nayak led the Bedas in refusal. They fought against British soldiers sent to collect weapons. British suppressed the rebellion with overwhelming force. Ramappa Nayak and leaders were executed. Significance: One of earliest armed revolts in Karnataka. Shows how British policies threatened traditional occupations. Part of broader 1857 resistance. Even common people ready to fight British injustice. Shows how Arms Act disarmed Indian resistance.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the importance and process of integration of princely states after Indian independence.
questions.correctAnswer: At independence in 1947, India had 562+ princely states. Challenge: States had to choose between India, Pakistan or independence. Sardar Patel as Deputy PM and Home Minister led integration. Used diplomacy, persuasion and firmness. Most states signed Instrument of Accession willingly. Special cases: Hyderabad — Nizam wanted independence. Operation Polo (1948) forced integration. Kashmir — Maharaja Hari Singh initially neutral. Pakistan-backed tribal invasion forced accession to India (October 1947). Junagadh — Plebiscite brought it to India. Significance: Created unified India. Prevented Balkanization. States reorganized linguistically (States Reorganization Act 1956). Essential for democracy and development. Patel's achievement in 1-2 years was remarkable.
questions.explanation: At independence in 1947, India had 562+ princely states. Challenge: States had to choose between India, Pakistan or independence. Sardar Patel as Deputy PM and Home Minister led integration. Used diplomacy, persuasion and firmness. Most states signed Instrument of Accession willingly. Special cases: Hyderabad — Nizam wanted independence. Operation Polo (1948) forced integration. Kashmir — Maharaja Hari Singh initially neutral. Pakistan-backed tribal invasion forced accession to India (October 1947). Junagadh — Plebiscite brought it to India. Significance: Created unified India. Prevented Balkanization. States reorganized linguistically (States Reorganization Act 1956). Essential for democracy and development. Patel's achievement in 1-2 years was remarkable.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the problems of poverty in India and the government measures to alleviate poverty.
questions.correctAnswer: Poverty characteristics: Large population below poverty line. Concentrated in rural areas. Affects SCs, STs disproportionately. Causes: Unequal wealth distribution. Unemployment. Illiteracy. Caste and gender discrimination. Population pressure. Effects: Malnutrition. Child labour. Crime. Economic underdevelopment. Government measures: MGNREGA (100 days employment). Pradhan Mantri Jan Dhan Yojana (financial inclusion). National Food Security Act (subsidized food). Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (housing). Ujjwala Yojana (LPG for BPL families). Ayushman Bharat (health insurance). Skill India Mission. PM Kisan Samman Nidhi.
questions.explanation: Poverty characteristics: Large population below poverty line. Concentrated in rural areas. Affects SCs, STs disproportionately. Causes: Unequal wealth distribution. Unemployment. Illiteracy. Caste and gender discrimination. Population pressure. Effects: Malnutrition. Child labour. Crime. Economic underdevelopment. Government measures: MGNREGA (100 days employment). Pradhan Mantri Jan Dhan Yojana (financial inclusion). National Food Security Act (subsidized food). Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (housing). Ujjwala Yojana (LPG for BPL families). Ayushman Bharat (health insurance). Skill India Mission. PM Kisan Samman Nidhi.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Describe the role of Gandhiji in India's freedom struggle from 1920 to 1947.
questions.correctAnswer: Gandhian Era (1920-1947): Non-Cooperation Movement (1920-22): First mass civil disobedience. Boycott of British goods, courts, schools. Suspended after Chauri Chaura violence (1922). Salt March/Civil Disobedience (1930): 241-mile Dandi March to break Salt Law. International attention drawn to India. Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931). Round Table Conferences (1930-32): Represented India in London. Poona Pact (1932) with Ambedkar on separate electorate. Quit India Movement (1942): 'Do or Die' slogan. Mass arrest of all leaders. Underground resistance continued. Partition and Independence (1947): Worked for Hindu-Muslim unity. Opposed partition but accepted to prevent civil war. Anti-violence work in Noakhali and Calcutta during communal riots. Assassination (January 30, 1948): Martyred by Nathuram Godse. Philosophy: Non-violence (Ahimsa) and Truth (Satyagraha) as weapons. Mobilized millions of ordinary Indians. Made independence movement truly national.
questions.explanation: Gandhian Era (1920-1947): Non-Cooperation Movement (1920-22): First mass civil disobedience. Boycott of British goods, courts, schools. Suspended after Chauri Chaura violence (1922). Salt March/Civil Disobedience (1930): 241-mile Dandi March to break Salt Law. International attention drawn to India. Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931). Round Table Conferences (1930-32): Represented India in London. Poona Pact (1932) with Ambedkar on separate electorate. Quit India Movement (1942): 'Do or Die' slogan. Mass arrest of all leaders. Underground resistance continued. Partition and Independence (1947): Worked for Hindu-Muslim unity. Opposed partition but accepted to prevent civil war. Anti-violence work in Noakhali and Calcutta during communal riots. Assassination (January 30, 1948): Martyred by Nathuram Godse. Philosophy: Non-violence (Ahimsa) and Truth (Satyagraha) as weapons. Mobilized millions of ordinary Indians. Made independence movement truly national.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the causes and consequences of the Second World War.
questions.correctAnswer: Causes: Rise of Fascism (Mussolini in Italy) and Nazism (Hitler in Germany). Failure of League of Nations. Treaty of Versailles humiliated Germany. Great Depression (1929) led to extremism. Appeasement policy by Britain and France. Japan's aggression in Asia. Immediate cause: Germany's invasion of Poland (September 1, 1939). World War II (1939-1945): Allies (UK, France, USA, USSR, India) vs Axis Powers (Germany, Italy, Japan). Key events: Fall of France (1940). Battle of Britain. German invasion of USSR (1941). Pearl Harbor — USA joins (1941). D-Day (1944). Atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki (1945). Germany surrenders May 1945. Japan surrenders September 1945. Consequences: 70-85 million deaths. Holocaust — 6 million Jews killed. Germany divided East and West. United Nations formed (1945). USA and USSR emerged as superpowers — Cold War. Decolonization wave — India independent 1947. Marshall Plan rebuilt Europe. NATO formed. Nuclear age began.
questions.explanation: Causes: Rise of Fascism (Mussolini in Italy) and Nazism (Hitler in Germany). Failure of League of Nations. Treaty of Versailles humiliated Germany. Great Depression (1929) led to extremism. Appeasement policy by Britain and France. Japan's aggression in Asia. Immediate cause: Germany's invasion of Poland (September 1, 1939). World War II (1939-1945): Allies (UK, France, USA, USSR, India) vs Axis Powers (Germany, Italy, Japan). Key events: Fall of France (1940). Battle of Britain. German invasion of USSR (1941). Pearl Harbor — USA joins (1941). D-Day (1944). Atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki (1945). Germany surrenders May 1945. Japan surrenders September 1945. Consequences: 70-85 million deaths. Holocaust — 6 million Jews killed. Germany divided East and West. United Nations formed (1945). USA and USSR emerged as superpowers — Cold War. Decolonization wave — India independent 1947. Marshall Plan rebuilt Europe. NATO formed. Nuclear age began.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the principles of India's foreign policy and India's contribution to world peace.
questions.correctAnswer: Foreign Policy Principles: 1. Non-Alignment: India does not join any military bloc. Founded NAM (1961) with Nasser (Egypt) and Tito (Yugoslavia). 2. Panchsheel (1954 with China): Five principles — mutual respect for sovereignty; non-aggression; non-interference; equality and mutual benefit; peaceful coexistence. 3. World Peace: India opposed nuclear weapons. Led disarmament campaigns. 4. Anti-Colonialism: Supported liberation movements. 5. Support to UN: Peacekeeping troops. India's Contribution: UN peacekeeping missions. South-South cooperation. SAARC — regional cooperation. Nehru's diplomacy during Korean War. Indo-Soviet Treaty (1971). Climate change cooperation. IBSA forum (India-Brazil-South Africa). Disaster relief to neighbours.
questions.explanation: Foreign Policy Principles: 1. Non-Alignment: India does not join any military bloc. Founded NAM (1961) with Nasser (Egypt) and Tito (Yugoslavia). 2. Panchsheel (1954 with China): Five principles — mutual respect for sovereignty; non-aggression; non-interference; equality and mutual benefit; peaceful coexistence. 3. World Peace: India opposed nuclear weapons. Led disarmament campaigns. 4. Anti-Colonialism: Supported liberation movements. 5. Support to UN: Peacekeeping troops. India's Contribution: UN peacekeeping missions. South-South cooperation. SAARC — regional cooperation. Nehru's diplomacy during Korean War. Indo-Soviet Treaty (1971). Climate change cooperation. IBSA forum (India-Brazil-South Africa). Disaster relief to neighbours.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the partition of India and its consequences.
questions.correctAnswer: Partition of India (1947): Background: Two-nation theory — Muslim League (Jinnah) demanded separate Pakistan. Direct Action Day (1946) — communal violence. Mountbatten Plan (3 June 1947) announced partition. Radcliffe Commission demarcated border in 40 days. India and Pakistan became independent Aug 14-15, 1947. Punjab and Bengal divided. Consequences: Mass migration: 12-17 million people displaced — largest human migration in history. Communal violence: Hundreds of thousands killed during partition riots. Refugee crisis. Economic disruption — industries separated from raw materials. Kashmir dispute — Maharaja Hari Singh's late accession led to India-Pakistan war (1947). Three wars between India and Pakistan (1947, 1965, 1971). Bangladesh created (1971) — East Pakistan separated. Partition remains one of most tragic events of 20th century.
questions.explanation: Partition of India (1947): Background: Two-nation theory — Muslim League (Jinnah) demanded separate Pakistan. Direct Action Day (1946) — communal violence. Mountbatten Plan (3 June 1947) announced partition. Radcliffe Commission demarcated border in 40 days. India and Pakistan became independent Aug 14-15, 1947. Punjab and Bengal divided. Consequences: Mass migration: 12-17 million people displaced — largest human migration in history. Communal violence: Hundreds of thousands killed during partition riots. Refugee crisis. Economic disruption — industries separated from raw materials. Kashmir dispute — Maharaja Hari Singh's late accession led to India-Pakistan war (1947). Three wars between India and Pakistan (1947, 1965, 1971). Bangladesh created (1971) — East Pakistan separated. Partition remains one of most tragic events of 20th century.
